464da9fa87b033fbc74a.text:
**Gaps — no SOP yet**

- **Member onboarding** – no documented first-touch-to-first-class journey (assessment form, locker setup, app access, coach intro, expectation-setting)
- **Cancellation & churn** – no win-back or exit process; you're losing members without a recorded reason or retention attempt
- **Member check-ins** – no scheduled touchpoint protocol (when, who, frequency, escalation if disengagement detected)
- **Health & safety incident reporting** – no template or escalation path for injuries, near-misses, equipment failures
- **Financial controls** – no SOP for refunds, payment failures, billing disputes, or member credit management
- **Complaints handling** – no documented protocol for member complaints (logging, response time, resolution, follow-up)
- **Coaching standards audit** – no quality assurance process; Class Quality Audit Checklist exists but no scheduling/ownership SOP
- **Pause/freeze membership** – no clear process for pausing (duration, auto-restart, comms, pricing)
- **Supply ordering & inventory** – Order Supplies & Sundries missing; no reorder levels, supplier contacts, or stock tracking
- **New staff onboarding** – no structured coach/admin induction (rotas, systems access, brand standards, safeguarding)

---

**Needs updating**

- **Coach SOPs (20/24)** – likely missing: performance expectations, escalation for underperformance, one-to-one coaching protocols, retention/career progression
- **Marketing SOPs (5/5)** – probably absent or vague: referral programme SOP (you have the title but no process), new quarter campaign timeline, member outcomes reporting for testimonials
- **Member data/GDPR** – critical gap; no documented retention policy, data deletion requests, or consent audit trail

ef78fa4eea1cbb63abd4.text:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO ACTION PLAN

## REVENUE — INCREASE NOW

• **Pricing drift is real — AR/M collapsed from £170.43 (Dec) to £120.48 (June).** That's a 29% drop. You've added 23 net members but revenue per member has fallen sharply. Audit your membership tiers: either members are downgrading to lower plans, or you're signing new clients on introductory rates and not graduating them. Implement a price rise on new sign-ups (aim back to £160+/member) and create a tiered upsell ladder: intro 1-month, then standard 12-month, then premium (PT sessions, nutrition, apparel bundles).

• **June revenue down £2,488 (12.3%) month-on-month despite member count up 19 to 147.** Churn dropped to 0% (excellent control), but the revenue lift is being strangled by lower AR/M. Single biggest priority: recover pricing discipline. Run a retention upsell campaign on your existing 147 members — offer class packs, merchandise, or annual renewal at a 5–10% premium to lock in higher LTV.

• **Non-peak hours are leaking revenue.** Identify your 3 quietest class slots (usually early morning, midweek afternoons). Run a targeted £5/class drop-in promotion to those cohorts for July—August (test 4 weeks, measure uptake). If uptake >15%, roll it into your standard schedule.

---

## EXPENSES — REDUCE & STABILISE

• **Non-people costs are volatile and spiking.** May: £9,233 → June: £9,390 (stable-ish), but Jan–April averaged £11k+. Post-move, your doubled-size studio will have higher utilities and maintenance. Benchmark: non-people should run 35–40% of revenue. June is 53% (£9,390 ÷ £17,711). Get three quotes on utilities, cleaning, and equipment maintenance this month. Target: bring non-people costs to £7,000–£7,500 by end of Q3, even if active clients hit 160+.

• **People costs are creeping (£3,686 in June vs £3,550 in May).** With 147 members, staffing should be efficient. Clarify: are you running extra classes to fill the new studio? If so, justify each shift by ARR contribution. If not, review whether you're overstaffed. Target: cap people costs at 18–20% of revenue. June

f527670fd10b0672288e.text:
• **Move Grace's Build: Lower (17 sessions) to JoJo.** JoJo is running 133 sessions and has established strength here; this is JoJo's highest-volume class anyway. Eilis could take 6–8 of these to develop, but primary load on JoJo.

• **Redistribute Grace's Elevate (6 sessions) and Strongevity (3 sessions) between JoJo and Eilis.** Eilis is under-loaded at 48 sessions (vs JoJo's 133) and both classes suit her current level; this grows Eilis's portfolio safely.

• **Hand Grace's No Sweat Intro (3 sessions) to any coach running that programme,** or consolidate into one coach's responsibility to reduce fragmentation.

• **Strip out the 29 Body Composition Scans (Grace 9, JoJo 20, Eilis 11) from coaching load entirely.** These are admin, not coaching—schedule them as standalone 15-min slots or member self-service where possible. This instantly frees ~10 hours from the team.

• **Shift 10–15 of JoJo's PT sessions to Eilis** (JoJo carries 44, Eilis carries 11). JoJo is stretched; Eilis has room and needs experience. Start with lower-complexity clients or pair-shadowing.

• **Confirm Eilis's class ceiling before the next month:** she's taking Elevate, Full Body, Plate, Capacity—ensure no Tuesday creep and that she's not overwhelmed before loading more from Grace.

c17ea6e513949b5bd341.text:
**For quiet studio time gaps:**

1. **Add a 30-min "Open Gym + Coach" slot on Monday and Thursday evenings** — staff a coach to supervise lifts and answer form questions during the currently dead space between classes, positioned as a lower-pressure alternative to full classes for members who want flexibility.

2. **Run a paid "Barbell Fundamentals" workshop (45 mins) on Saturday mornings before the quieter weekend timetable** — target Barbelle and Strongevity members who may be intimidated; position it as a confidence-builder that feeds into those underattended classes.

**For the quieter classes themselves:**

3. **Move Engine to a Tuesday or Wednesday slot** (your busiest days) and pair it with a 2-week member challenge: "Complete 3 Engine classes, get a free Elevate drop-in" — this leverages momentum from Build classes and gives Engine a visibility boost through a high-traffic day rather than its current softer placement.

47b0548900970eee6400.text:
# Service Menu Execution for The Forge

**Audit what you're ACTUALLY selling right now**
- Have each coach list every service/variation they've sold in the past 3 months (classes, PT sessions, nutrition plans, challenges, etc.). You'll likely find inconsistency—different coaches quoting different prices or inclusions. This is your real starting point, not a blank slate.

**Price from member willingness, not cost-plus guessing**
- Survey 15-20 current members: "What would you pay for [PT package]? At what price would you think it's overpriced?" Use their answers as anchors, not your assumptions. For women's fitness in Belfast, you're competing on perceived value and community—not undercutting big chains.

**Build TWO menus: internal (coaching clarity) + external (sales/website)**
- Internal: Include profit margins, what coaches can/can't bundle, upgrade paths (e.g., "member gets 4 free PT consultations to upsell ongoing sessions"). External: Remove pricing complexity. Strip it to 3-4 core packages max—people freeze with too many options.

**Create a one-page "service conversation script" for coaches**
- Coaches need to know the tier-appropriate next step for each member type (new vs. loyal vs. lapsed). Without this, they'll either oversell or undersell. A simple decision tree ("Member doing classes 3x/week → offer this") drives consistency and revenue.

**Publish and test within 2 weeks**
- Get it live internally first. Let coaches use it for 14 days, collect friction points, then refine. Don't perfectionism-delay this—feedback from real conversations

e4aeda2aa0d1508a8f79.text:
**Insights**

Your cost per lead has collapsed 90% (£21 to £2) and volume has surged 8x, but your close rate has cratered from 59% (Apr) to 4% (Jul). You're flooding the funnel with cheap, cold traffic that isn't converting—this is a classic quality-versus-quantity trap. The profit is still positive this month (£947), but you're essentially burning cash on unqualified leads; at April's 29% close rate, those 165 leads would've generated 48 sales and £7,000+ profit instead of £1,323.

Something structural changed in July—either your targeting widened dramatically, your creative softened, or the algorithm shifted to volume-focused campaigns. The leads are real (you got them) but they're not your people. You've optimised for the metric (CPL) rather than the outcome (sales).

**Actions**

1. **Immediately audit and tighten your targeting.** Pull your July audiences in Ads Manager—what changed? Did you expand to Lookalike, broaden age ranges, or drop exclusions? Revert to April's targeting spec (interest stacks, age band, location radius, income if available) as your baseline. Run a small parallel test (£50/day) with April's exact setup to confirm it still converts at 25%+ close rate.

2. **Pause any broad or automated targeting campaigns in July and rebuild on intent signals.** Test three audiences next month: (a) fitness/gym interests + female + 25–45 + Belfast/commutable radius; (b) past website visitors + video viewers; (c) a tight Lookalike built from your April sales (not leads). Allocate 50% budget to (a), 30% to (b), 20% to (c).

3. **Resurrect April's creative narrative.** July's £2-lead ads are pulling clicks but not commitment. What was April saying? Run it again, verbatim if possible, and A/B against a "social proof / testimonial" creative (use your 20 sales from Apr/May as case studies). July's creative is likely vague ("Join now!") vs April's specificity ("6-week

fbb52f88c48e54607ffa.text:
# The Forge — Fractional CFO Review

## REVENUE — BIGGEST PRIORITY: Arrest the churn spike and stabilise member quality

• **Churn spiked to 12.7% in May (vs 3.3% baseline in June & April).** You gained 13 net members (122→135) but lost 17 to churn — that's waste. Post-relocation churn is often class timetable shock or retention gap. **Action: audit May/June class schedule adherence, survey dropped members, and run a 4-week "win-back" campaign (free trial week for lapsed members) in July. Target: drop churn back to ≤5%.**

• **Rev/member is volatile (£115–£138 range).** June at £115 is the lowest in 8 months despite 137 active members. This suggests either discounting creep or product mix shift (fewer premium cards, more base tier). **Action: audit your pricing mix in TeamUp — enforce minimum membership tier or upsell class packs (e.g. "8-class top-up" at £35) to June cohort. Target: £130 minimum rev/member.**

• **April's relocation killed cash flow (−£6,819) but didn't dent revenue long-term.** Post-move revenue held at £15.7–£16.9k (vs pre-move £14.2–£16.9k). **Action: use the larger studio footprint now open — identify 2–3 quiet class slots (check TeamUp attendance logs) and fill them via targeted ads to lapsed members and local corporates. A single added class at 60% utilisation = ~£600–£800/month incremental.**

---

## EXPENSES — Cash burn in March (−£5,307) and June (−£2,521) signals timing/payroll misalignment

• **Cash out spiked March (£23,328) and April (£39,015); June still elevated at £20,644 vs revenue of £15,765.** March and June are ~£6–8k overspends vs revenue. **Action: pull your P&L by cost category (payroll, rent, supplies, utilities). Likely culprit: bi-weekly or monthly payroll hitting in those weeks, plus relocation stock purchases in April. Confirm cash-out buckets with your accountant and implement a 13-week rolling cash forecast to avoid surprises.**

• **April's relocation spend (£39k cash out) was pre-planned; your June elevation (£20.6k) is not. June's cash out is
